data.title: Fed's Wait And See Stance Could Persist Through To September
data.description:
By James Knightley, Padhraic Garvey, CFA, & Francesco Pesole

Fed leaves rates unchanged, but highlights elevated uncertainty

The Federal Reserve has left monetary policy unchanged with the Fed funds target rate range remaining at 4.25-4.50%. It was a unanimous decision with the accompanying press release stating that the economy continues to "expand at a solid pace", labour market conditions remain "solid" while inflation "remains somewhat elevated". All this phraseology is the same as last time.

The key modifications are that the Fed believes the "uncertainty about the economic outlook has increased further” and that the “risks of higher unemployment and higher inflation have risen". There is nothing particularly surprising in these comments with limited market reaction.

The 'wait and see' stance could continue for another couple of meetings

President Trump and Treasury Secretary Bessent will keep pressuring the Fed to cut interest rates, but those demands will continue to fall on deaf ears as officials try to gauge the inflationary impact from the Administration’s trade policies amidst ongoing labour market strength. Higher tariffs look set to lift prices while port operators and logistics firms are warning of a potential supply crunch that risks amplifying the near-term inflation threat. As such the Fed is in 'wait and see' mode, with Chair Jay Powell warning last month that “our obligation is to keep longer-term inflation expectations well anchored and to make certain that a one-time increase in the price level does not become an ongoing inflation problem”. This was repeated at today's press conference.

Consumer confidence readings point to the risk of a steep drop in spending

Fed cuts may come later, but end up being sharper

However, the scale of the slump in consumer and corporate sentiment to levels historically consistent with recession will be of concern to the Fed. Economic uncertainty and government spending curbs mean that trade deals and tax cuts need to be agreed quickly to prevent a stagflation infused downturn. Nonetheless, we expect that shelter-related disinflation, as already hinted at by the Cleveland Fed’s new tenant rent series, will give the Fed the room to respond with rate cuts later in the year. The market favours a July start point, but we see the risk for slippage and it may be that the Fed kicks things off with a 50bp cut in September, just as they did in 2024.

No material comment on the tapering process, and bonds trade both sides of the rate cut outlook

The main immediate impulse from the preliminary headlines was lower rates and a steeper curve, with most of the action coming in the real rates space. From that, there was a supposition that the Fed is primed to react to higher unemployment and hold its nose to rising price rise risks. That was quite the reaction, some brief and in fact balanced snippets from the press release. That type of price reaction needed to be validated from the press conference. In fact the commentary at the press conference was not quite in sync with the early price action, and market rates edged back up again. Overall, the net outcome is slightly lower for market rates, and in our opinion there is an avenue for that to be extrapolated ahead on a theory that we see some macro pressures build. The 10yr yield can easily get down to the 4% area in such a scenario. But for now, the contemporaneous economy is just about firm enough to validate market rates staying about where they are for now.

Nothing new on the bond roll-off programme (quantitative tightening). Last time, the Fed lowered the cap on the roll-off of Treasuries to $5bn per month, which is effectively zero. In turn that means that the Fed continues to be a net buyer of Treasuries, as the roll-off ranges from $20bn to $60bn per month. No change to this policy. At the same time a $35bn cap remains on the MBS roll-off, but we’ve not been hitting that cap, which means that MBS bonds that mature do not get re-invested. Anything in excess of $35bn would be re-invested in Treasuries (which is in fact rare). Long term, the Fed would have an ambition to take the MBS bonds off its books completely, and ideally replace them with Treasuries. No (new) commentary on this today though. Clearly all this being left to another occasion, perhaps awaiting less impactful times. For now the slower tapering process continues, and can do so as bank reserves remain ample enough. One technical help here comes from the debt ceiling, as until this is raised or suspended, the US Treasury is spending down and in so doing, adding to bank reserves.

FX not focused on the Fed for the moment

The dollar is slightly offered after the FOMC announcement, but the FX impact has been quite contained on the whole. Short-term rate differentials have had very limited spillover into USD-crosses of late, and the dollar was probably hit more by the Fed's concerns for a rise in both unemployment and inflation rather than any hawkish/dovish takeaways today. After all, the run-in to this FOMC meeting had seen a nearly 20 hawkish repricing in the USD OIS curve, but an underwhelming dollar rebound.

The dollar continues to embed a sizeable risk premium relative to its usual market drivers (rates and equity differentials, global risk sentiment). In EUR/USD that translates into around 4% overvaluation in our estimates, but the path to make markets comfortable with a substantially smaller risk premium isn't going to be smooth. A constant flow of positive news on trade risk de-escalation is necessary, but probably not sufficient in the face of the damage markets think tariffs are already inflicting on the US economy.

We think there will be sustained support around the 1.1250-1.130 area in EUR/USD in the near term: that has proven to be the region where most dip-buyers emerge. The balance of risks is still skewed to the upside for the pair.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

data.title: Nvidia: I Am Buying The YTD Weakness With Confidence
data.description:
Investment Thesis

My previous bullish Nvidia (NASDAQ:NVDA) thesis did not age quite well, as the stock is currently 15% cheaper than it was on February 21. The recent share price weakness is explained by the broader market's sentiment though. I am confident because recent developments reinforce a bullish outlook and suggest that fundamentals remain rock-solid. Moreover, the stock is extremely attractively valued, and AI tailwinds keep fortifying. All in all, I reiterate a "Strong Buy" rating for NVDA.

Recent Developments

Nvidia released its latest quarterly earnings on February 26. There was a confident dual beat against consensus estimates, which allowed the company to continue its strong earnings surprise record. The FQ4 revenue grew by a staggering 78% YoY, which is absolutely jaw-dropping for a company of NVDA's scale. The bottom-line growth trend also remained impressive, with the YoY adjusted EPS increase from $0.52 to $0.89.

As usual, I pay a lot of attention to margins, as they speak volumes. Looking at the gross and operating margin dynamics is vital for investors, as they give insights into the company's ability to exercise the economies of scale effect [or the operating leverage]. Strong operating leverage is vital for shareholder value creation, as it increases chances of further profitability expansion alongside future revenue growth.

Nvidia's gross margin has demonstrated solid improvement over the last two years, reaching a sky-high level of 75%. As a result, Nvidia has vast resources to invest in innovation and marketing, which fortifies the company's potential to maintain robust revenue growth for longer. Nvidia's profitability metrics are not only miles ahead of the sector median, but are also unrivaled compared to the "Magnificent 7" companies. The one that I like the most is the net income per employee, which is above $2 million for NVDA while no other achieved even a million-dollar mark.

As a result, NVDA's balance sheet is a fortress and positions the company well to fuel multiple potential growth drivers. A large cash pile together with massive FCF generation capacity means that NVDA is able to drive a holistic growth strategy, which will include both R&D and M&A.

The upcoming earnings release is scheduled for the last week of May, and Nvidia shows no signs of the growth momentum deteriorating. The expected-by-consensus 65% YoY revenue growth is massive, and the EPS is forecasted to follow the top-line with a 45% growth compared to the same quarter of FY 2025. The fact that there were more upward EPS revisions over the last 90 days is a bullish indicator suggesting that Wall Street analysts are confident in positive earnings surprise and confident guidance from the management.

It is not a secret that Nvidia's massive revenue growth momentum is explained by the global accelerated adoption of AI capabilities. Therefore, looking at industry developments is vital to understand the potential longevity of Nvidia's success.

The largest tech companies already reported their Q1 earnings, and there were several bullish signs for Nvidia. All three largest global cloud industry players demonstrated strong revenue growth and profitability expansion in Q1 2025. Among them are Amazon [AWS], Microsoft [Azure], and Google [Google Cloud]. These three behemoths account for almost two-thirds of the global cloud market, meaning that it will be reasonable to extrapolate their performance to the entire industry. As all three of them demonstrated robust double-digit YoY revenue growth, it is highly likely that the entire industry will continue to thrive.

What is the most important for Nvidia is that all three cloud behemoths reiterated their aggressive AI capital spending plans for 2025. This looks reasonable because as long as the industry ensures double-digit revenue growth and profitability expansion, cloud giants will continue to seek new opportunities to expand their footprints in a rapidly growing domain.

Recent developments around Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (TSM), the world's largest chip fab, also indicate that the industry remains hot. The Taiwanese manufacturing giant continues aggressively expanding its manufacturing footprint in the U.S., which is certainly a bullish sign for the entire industry. In addition, analysts from Morgan Stanley recently concluded that the demand for CoWoS [Chip on Wafer on Substrate] remains rock-solid.

Another large manufacturer of data center components, Foxconn (OTCPK:FXCOF), also recently added optimism with their strong Q2 2025 outlook. FXCOF delivered a robust MoM and YoY revenue growth, which was mostly explained by the strong demand for their "Cloud and Networking Products" business.

With all that being said, AI tailwinds remain strong. As a company that dominates the graphics processing unit [GPU] with a 92% market share, Nvidia is extremely likely to continue capitalizing on the AI spending spree. The company's closest rival in the AI accelerators' field at the moment is Advanced Micro Devices (AMD). Nvidia protects its competitive edge by aggressively investing in R&D. On the TTM basis, Nvidia's R&D spending is twice as high as AMD's. As I mentioned earlier, Nvidia's profitability capacity is unparalleled, and the company's balance sheet is a fortress. Therefore, the company is firmly positioned to protect its moat against AMD further.

From the seasonality perspective, the setup also looks quite favorable. Nvidia's win rates in January-April have been historically quite volatile over the last decade. At the same time, the May-November timeframe is usually quite strong with win rates varying from 70% to 80%. This is another robust bullish catalyst for the next few months, in my opinion.

Valuation Update

Nvidia's valuation ratios look extremely attractive, as they are currently significantly lower compared to the company's historical averages. It is vital to highlight that we speak about ratios across the board. In addition, a very aggressive P/E contraction is forecasted by consensus. Therefore, valuation ratios clearly indicate notable undervaluation.

I need the discount rate to proceed with a DCF model for Nvidia. The company's almost debt-free capital structure clearly indicates that I can confidently use cost of equity as a discount rate. This one is calculated easily with the help of the CAPM formula as all the variables are available on the Internet.

The FCF margin assumption is another one that needs some calculations. As a conservative investor, I use an ex-stock-based compensation [ex-SBC] assumption for the FCF margin. It equals 30.3% for NVDA, which I used as the first-year assumption. For years beyond, a 100-bps yearly expansion is included because of NVDA's historically strong operating leverage.

Since we see that there is a thin layer of uncertainty related to President Trump's tariffs, I use a much more conservative 4% perpetual growth rate this time. Nvidia's FY2027 [which is mostly calendar 2026] revenue assumption is $247.87 billion. For the first scenario, I used a moderate 10% revenue CAGR for the next seven years.

With a 10% revenue CAGR and a 4% perpetual growth rate, NVDA's fair value [as a business] is close to $4.1 trillion. This is 46% higher than the company's current $2.8 trillion market cap, meaning that the stock is 46% undervalued. The margin of safety is huge because even with a 5% revenue CAGR, Nvidia's fair value of the business remains above $3 trillion.

To summarize my two scenarios, there is a table which readers can see below. I think that for a company like NVDA, the first scenario is absolutely doable and achievable. Therefore, my target price estimate is $166 for NVDA. The second scenario is demonstrated to show readers how deeply this stock is undervalued.

Risks Update

NVDA's weak YTD performance revealed how vulnerable this stock is to the broader market's sentiment. Despite the company's rock-solid fundamentals and strong AI tailwinds, the stock is down 15% in 2025. Therefore, in case of new adverse geopolitical developments that might spook investors once again, NVDA is also likely to plunge.

Competition risks are intensifying for Nvidia. While I am confident that AMD is unlikely to ever close the gap with Nvidia, there are Chinese players like Huawei. This company already created fierce competition for Apple in the smartphone market, which underscores Huawei's competitive edge. The Chinese giant is reportedly set to roll out a new powerful AI chip, which might potentially undermine Nvidia's dominance in the space. In addition, Nvidia's CEO recently said that China is "not far behind" the U.S. in AI capabilities.

Insiders continue to sell their NVDA stocks. There were no purchases from them over the last twelve months, which is difficult to call a positive catalyst. On the other hand, insiders are consistently awarded with new shares as part of their SBC, which might offset the need to purchase the stock in the market.

Bottom Line

All in all, I firmly believe that NVDA remains a "Strong Sell". The developments are mostly extremely positive, and the stock is very attractively valued. I continue to dollar-average my position, which appears to be the most prudent approach in the current uncertain environment.

data.title: Super Micro Computer Q3 Earnings Review: Uninvestable Might Be An Understatement
data.description:
As we had predicted in our previous coverage on the stock, Super Micro Computer, Inc.’s (NASDAQ:SMCI) early-year upsurge was poised to dissipate. Recall that the stock had more than doubled in the two weeks following its announcement of a $700 million convertible top-up and a $40 billion FY26 revenue guidance in early February. We had viewed both as short-lived distractions at the time, given that SMCI’s underlying accounting and operational challenges were bound to expose the stock’s early-year appreciation to weakness.

This was evident in the stock’s subsequent selloff since late February, and further corroborated by the company’s weak financial results for the March quarter— its first earnings update since regaining compliance with Nasdaq filing rules. Despite the alleviation of delisting and subsequent liquidity risks after SMCI had submitted its long-overdue FY24 and fiscal 1H25 filings in February, there remains much work to be done before the company restores its credibility. The latest earnings disappointment has only worsened the situation by serving as an added force against its turnaround efforts.

Paired with looming external challenges that include supply chain and regulatory uncertainties, the risk backdrop remains skewed to the downside for SMCI. This is expected to put its $40 billion FY26 revenue outlook—which markets had initially been optimistic about—further out of reach, removing a key fundamental pillar to the stock’s recovery prospects.

Fiscal Q3 Shortfall Puts FY26 Guidance at Risk

It’s become increasingly clear that SMCI’s $40 billion sales guidance for FY26 disclosed in early February was a distraction to the ongoing turmoil experienced in the underlying business. This was corroborated by the company’s slashed FY25 guidance to $21.8 billion to $22.6 billion, which fell below the lower range of the previously offered outlook.

Management’s reiterated confidence that shipment volumes will ramp up in the June and September quarters with improving Blackwell supply from Nvidia (NVDA)—but we’ve heard this explanation for at least three consecutive quarters now to no avail:

George Wang

Okay, guys. Thanks for taking my question. I have two quick ones. Firstly, can you kind of double-click on which quarter do you think you will start booking the Blackwell revenue? Last time, you guys alluded to sometime in the June quarter. Just curious whether that is still on track. Just any kind of high level in terms of when do you think the Blackwell is going to show up in the P&L?

Charles Liang

Yes, very big question. Indeed, we're asking NVIDIA every day. So, I hope their production can go smoothly and go for high volume very soon. And once they have a chip available, our solutions are fully ready.

Source: SMCI F1Q25 Business Update Transcript.

Quinn Bolton: Hi. Thanks for taking my question. Just wanted to follow up on the GB200 NVL72 question. Just sounds like you guys are ready to go, but the biggest gating factor is just support from NVIDIA. Do you guys have a forecast from NVIDIA? You know, when you think you're going to start to see, you know, supply the GPU, so that you can ship the NVL72, where visibility is still pretty low on availability of the GPUs?

Charles Liang: We already proved pretty much everything. And now, just waiting for—and we are in some allocation, some volume, but the volume demand is way, much bigger. So, we are waiting for more allocation. So hopefully, very soon we can ship in much higher volume.

Source: SMCI F2Q25 Business Update Transcript.

Charles Liang: As previously announced, our fiscal Q3 net revenue totaled $4.6 billion, coming in lower than our original forecast. This decline was primarily due to customers waiting and evaluating AI platforms between the current Hopper and the upcoming Blackwell GPUs, leading to delayed commitments. We expect many of these engagements to materialize in the June and September quarters, strengthening our confidence in meeting our long-term growth targets as we close out this eventful fiscal year.

Source: SMCI F3Q25 Earnings Call Transcript.

Not only has management slashed the FY25 guidance, but there’s also been conflicting commentary on whether the $40 billion FY26 revenue outlook still holds:

Michael Ng

Hi, good afternoon. Thank you very much for the question. I was just wondering if you could talk a little bit more about your demand outlook. Are you, and apologies if I missed it, but are you reiterating the $40 billion revenue target for fiscal 2026, and perhaps you can talk a little bit about any changes that you might be seeing from a demand perspective, aside from the timing of the technology platform transition and the macro and uncertainty. Thank you.

Charles Liang

Yes, we remain very confident in our midterm and long-term growth. So, especially the Blackwell product line, we have a very strong demand, and also our coming soon DC BBS data center building blocks total solution. We see lots of customers really interested in our data center total solution. So demand growth will keep strong, and yes, the tariff and some macroeconomic uncertainty. We, at this moment, do not provide guidance for fiscal year 2026. But when it's the visibility becomes clearer, we will share at that time.

Source: SMCI F3Q25 Earnings Call Transcript.

As discussed in our previous coverage, we’d highlighted how it’s unusually rare for companies to provide fundamental guidance that’s two years out (albeit FY26 just being a couple of quarters away for SMCI). While it’s common for companies to establish long-term growth targets, corporate management typically withholds a forward outlook before the current fiscal year is finished. This is often a consideration of management’s fiduciary duty to investors, given uncertainties in baseline results and evolving market dynamics—especially in the fast-paced R&D turnover typically observed in semiconductor technologies that SMCI depends materially on.

As a result, we’d viewed the preliminary guidance as a mere distraction, especially given the limited reliability of SMCI’s financial results due to its still outstanding audit report at the time. This was evident in CEO Charles Liang’s latest attempt to walk back on his previous confidence in reaching $40 billion in sales in FY26. The FY26 sales prospect would’ve represented 65% y/y growth based on the midpoint of SMCI’s previous FY25 guidance, and a tougher 80% y/y growth target based on the midpoint of its revised FY25 outlook today.

The reduced confidence in SMCI’s FY26 growth outlook, which management attributed to ongoing tariff uncertainties and product transition-induced purchase delays, is further corroborated by limited demand visibility in recent industry checks. Specifically, Taiwanese tech reporting outlet, WT Research, had recently suggested based on its industry checks that visibility into AI server shipments based on Nvidia’s Blackwell GB200 GPUs only extends until calendar 3Q25. There are also risks of further delays to next-generation GB300 systems simply due to their complexity and broader supply chain constraints. The majority of volumes are also accounted for by rivals Hon Hai (OTCPK:HNHAF, OTCPK:HNHPF) and Quanta (OTC:QUCCF).

This accordingly increases skepticism about SMCI’s ability to successfully capture incoming Blackwell deployment opportunities, which management expects to materialize in the June and September quarters. Paired with added demand concerns stemming from “tariff and some macroeconomic (uncertainties)” alluded by management, SMCI is likely to completely retract the $40 billion revenue guidance for FY26, which is a risk that shouldn’t be overlooked. If SMCI maintains the 65% y/y growth rate it had previously guided for FY26, the company’s expected to achieve ~$37 billion in FY26 revenues based on the midpoint of its revised FY25 sales outlook—a $4 billion headwind to the previously disclosed FY26 sales outlook which management had initially said was “conservative”:

Charles Liang: So this year, even 10-K today, we grew about 60%. Last year we grew 110%. So the coming year, fiscal 2026, at this moment, we believe at least, we will grow 65% at least. So that's, I believe, a very conservative estimation.

Source: SMCI F2Q25 Business Update Transcript.

SMCI’s expected fundamental deterioration not only raises the risk that its currently depressed valuation becomes the new base case, but also threatens its standing in the S&P 500 (SP500). The stock has dropped about 70% since its admission to the index, with SMCI’s underlying business fundamentals having weakened significantly over the same period due to the combination of accounting-related disruptions and company-specific execution mishaps. This, in turn, heightens the risk of waning institutional demand, further exacerbating SMCI’s vulnerability to multiple compression.

Further Margin Compression Awaits

In addition to growth uncertainties, SMCI also faces imminent margin compression headwinds. This is evident in the 2.2 percentage point sequential gross margin compression reported by SMCI for the fiscal third quarter, which management has attributed to inventory reserve—an allowance estimated for anticipated the inventory value write-offs, which is then realized into costs.

Specifically, inventory reserves are recorded as an anticipated loss to inventory value. In GAAP accounting, inventory must be reported on the balance sheet at the lower of net realizable value or cost; if the net realizable value is below the cost of producing the inventory, then the difference is written off as its asset with a matching impact to the income statement via impairment costs. Sometimes, companies will anticipate a write-off in advance based on industry dynamics and record a reserve allowance, which is then reported as a cost when the inventory is sold and the “write-off” is realized. Although CEO Charles Liang has expressed his confidence that the inventory reserve impact will reduce to under 100 bps in the June quarter and closer to zero by the September quarter during SMCI’s latest earnings call, significant uncertainties remain on the horizon that shouldn’t be overlooked.

The company has actually disclosed observations that customers are already “moving to the newer platforms in the upcoming quarters.” This means older inventory is poised to be sold at lower prices that could fall below cost and result in a write-off. The expectation is further corroborated by industry expectations for an earlier-than-expected retirement of Nvidia’s Blackwell GB200 GPUs with replacement by the next-generation Blackwell GB300 GPUs.

Recall that the GB200 go-to-market was largely plagued by output constraints due to the manufacturing complexities of the product. This included speculation of unresolved overheating issues, and Nvidia management’s subsequent confirmation of Blackwell yield issues during the product’s early production ramp-up, which have since been resolved with a change to the GPU mask. Due to the related issues and subsequent delays to GB200 system deployments, the industry is now expecting an earlier-than-expected transition to the GB300. Nvidia management had initially predicted the GB300 systems to start shipping by 3Q25 during GTC 2025, though there have also been emerging industry concerns of potential production delays.

The problem with fast-tracked adoption of the GB300 systems is that it’ll add to the gross margin pressures observed at SMCI during its fiscal third quarter. As discussed in the earlier section, SMCI’s been preparing racks for the GB200 systems—primarily the NVL72 server configuration—for at least three quarters now in anticipation of strong customer demand. Yet, the faster-than-expected transition to GB300 systems will inevitably upend that product roadmap. Specifically, the GB300 system is expected to deliver enhanced performance efficiencies through increased density and memory. And this will inadvertently come with a higher power requirement compared to its predecessor. What this means is that the server cabinets and racks that SMCI had initially prepared for the GB200 NVL72 won’t be transferable nor compatible with the upcoming GB300 without significant reconfiguration costs.

This risk was evident all along, though management has, time and again, downplayed the prospects of it actually happening. During the F2Q25 business update, analysts had actually raised questions over risks of extended sequential growth headwinds due to the impact of production transition on end customer demand. Yet in response, CEO Charles Liang had only focused his confidence on the strong FY25 and FY26 prospects, with limited means to mitigate the demand issue on hand that’s now materialized into reality:

Samik Chatterjee

Maybe just to start off, Charles, I think the Last time you mentioned, which was in 2024, that we could expect sequential revenue increases in the medium-term on a quarterly basis. When I sort of look back at it in hindsight, it looks like what derailed that sequential growth to some extent was the product transition from Nvidia in going from one product generation to another, which also drove some change in customer behavior.

As you're thinking about the revenue target here for $40 billion for fiscal 2026, I mean, is there an underlying assumption that you won't see a similar customer behavior change towards the next generation product as Nvidia goes through a transition again in that timeframe, or is there something that I'm missing in that sort of overall product transition that we should expect from your GPU supplier?

Charles Liang

Yeah, I mean, for calendar 2025, for example, I believe we should be pretty able to repeat 2023 history. In 2023, [H193] (ph) launched, and we are ahead of the competition. So we grew very well. In 2025, calendar year 2025, we are facing the same opportunity now, except before our old air cooler, and now it's Duke cooler. And in terms of liquid cooling, especially DLC, we have a major market share, and we have a huge capacity, 1500 racks per month capacity ready. And we already have many customers approved.

Source: SMCI F2Q25 Business Update Transcript.

As a result, it’s likely that SMCI won’t be able to monetize its existing inventory without significant reconfiguration costs. Alternatively, it’d need to significantly mark down its existing servers designed for the GB200 to clear obsolete inventory—meaning further write-offs. Either way, it’s going to be further pressure on gross margins, which leaves limited confidence in CEO Charles Liang’s remarks for inventory reserve reduction to zero by the September quarter.

Regulatory and Macroeconomic Wildcard

On top of anticipated fundamental weakness, SMCI also remains exposed to the adverse implications of company-specific and industrywide regulatory and macroeconomic uncertainties.

The upcoming decision on how the White House plans to implement the AI Diffusion Framework on May 13 remains a wildcard. And this will likely further limit visibility into SMCI’s near-to-medium-term growth outlook due to changes in export curbs. Specifically, SMCI generates about a fifth of its revenues from Asia. Although the company does not further disclose its Asia revenue mix by country, it’s likely that much of the related sales were generated from China and Singapore—key APAC growth markets observed for chipmakers like Nvidia, Intel (INTC), and AMD (AMD), which supplies the critical components fitted into SMCI’s servers. With both markets likely facing stricter export curbs under the new AI Diffusion Framework, the upcoming May 13th decision could further dampen SMCI’s growth outlook as the industry scrambles for a path forward.

SMCI also faces incremental margin compression risks due to the ongoing tariff negotiations and subsequent macroeconomic uncertainties. The industrywide headwind has received substantial airtime during SMCI’s latest earnings update, with management using it as the new non-company-specific reason for its anticipated underperformance. As discussed in the earlier section, the culprit was previously Nvidia Blackwell delays.

Samik Chatterjee

Previously, you have talked about margins improving as you start ramping new products, whereas you sound the guidance itself implies a bit more cautiousness on that front. Has there been a change in the pricing landscape for the new products that you're seeing in the market, or is this more about the tougher pricing environment for Hopper-based products?

Charles Liang

I think it's a combination of concern about tariffs and so conservatism there, and also with some, obviously also some impact from the changeover in technology platforms.

Source: SMCI F3Q25 Earnings Call Transcript.

Most importantly, aside from industrywide regulatory and macroeconomic headwinds, SMCI’s accounting overhang persists, which shouldn’t be overlooked. As discussed in our previous coverage on the stock, SMCI’s FY24 audit of internal controls over financial reporting (ICFR) had actually received an adverse opinion. This means there have been multiple material weaknesses identified in SMCI’s ICFR environment, which could result in material misstatements in its financial statements. Given the remediation process is ongoing (the PCAOB allows a one-year remediation period for ICFR deficiencies), the risk of ensuing material misstatements and subsequent restatements to prior period reports remain.

On top of this, the adverse opinion on SMCI’s FY24 ICFR environment, its high-profile and repeated offense of similar accounting issues and filing delinquencies last year, and its auditor BDO’s historical track record of audit deficiencies make the company’s latest year-end audit a prime candidate for PCAOB inspection. With the U.S. Senate having recently called out BDO’s eye-watering audit deficiency rate as a reason for greater scrutiny, the risks of PCAOB inspection for SMCI’s FY24 audit report are high. The implications include potential discoveries of material misstatements that will need prior period reports to be restated, which will result in greater margin pressure due to remediation costs and further dampen investors’ confidence. Any discovery of unresolved accounting issues would amplify SMCI’s exposure to litigation risks and associated costs, further pressuring its already weakened margins.

Final Thoughts

SMCI stock may seem cheap, trading at about 10.5x estimated earnings and 0.6x estimated sales on a relative basis to its peers. But the foregoing analysis on the breadth of fundamental and regulatory risks facing SMCI prescribes substance to the stock’s current discount, which could very well become its new base case at best.

The combination of mounting and unresolved headwinds effectively clouds visibility into Super Micro Computer, Inc.’s FY26 outlook, undermining earlier investor optimism around its recovery. Meanwhile, the anticipated execution challenges are likely to open the door for intensifying competition as next-generation solutions ramp up and erode SMCI’s earlier first-mover advantage in direct liquid-cooling (DLC) systems vital to next-generation AI infrastructure. Until there is greater evidence that SMCI can overcome these risks, which would at least take several quarters of consistent positive outperformance, the stock’s valuation discount may not just persist—it could deepen.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Energy Transfer: Weighing Preferred's 7.4% Yield Versus Bonds
data.description:
With all fixed income products, there is always the dreaded call option which the issuing company possesses. This has the ability to ruin a good thing and forces you to give up an income stream you rather hang on to for deal life. It also limits your upside when you buy fixed income securities. The "par" value, or the "par plus make whole provisions", is the maximum you will get. Hence, the securities that are not callable, get extra love. Energy Transfer LP 9.250% FXD PFD I (NYSE:ET.PR.I) is one such security that is not callable. For a good primer on their history, you can read our earlier work on this. Today, we will quickly recap the reasons investors love this and also look at some alternatives for the K-1 dodger.

Key Reasons To Love ET.PR.I

1) A very high yield on the original issue of 9.25%. This translates into a 7.4% yield on the current price.

2) The backing of Energy Transfer LP (NYSE:ET). There are few quality preferreds left in the midstream space and in our view, all are north of the US border. ET.PR.I is one real exception to this.

3) Significant upside in the case of return of ZIRP (Zero Interest Rate Policy). While that is not exactly something we think will happen, the security could easily trade with a 5.5% yield in that scenario and that would translate into a price of about $15.35.

Key Reason To Hate ET.PR.I

It has a K-1. That really it is. In fact, it is the only reason we don't own it. Canadians have an actual legitimate reason to not buy MLP related securities, as there is an extremely punitive 40% withholding tax on distributions AND proceeds of sale. Recovery of that tax is virtually impossible. For US citizens, the K-1 is not the most punitive aspect, and we think investors should not avoid securities that harbor a K-1. Nor should they pony up massive premiums to buy a Non-K-1 edition of a security. But the reality is that "does this have a K-1?" remains the number one comment on all our articles combined over the last 9 years. It even beats out "I am happy collecting my income as my shares go to zero." So the masses hate the K-1 and hence we will provide a couple of alternatives a little further down. But first, let's see if ET.PR.I is cheap.

Is ET.PR.I Cheap?

ET.PR.I carries a BB+ rating from Fitch (and S&P) and all investment decisions should start with evaluation in light of that.

How much can you get for a BB+ bond?

SPDR® Bloomberg High Yield Bond ETF (JNK) is a nice fund to look at as it has a medium duration, and you can see the yield to maturity right on the main page.

JNK right off is giving you a higher yield with a lower duration. After all ET.PR.I's duration is close to infinite as it cannot be called. But there is a catch here. JNK does not hold BB+ rated bonds. BB+ on Fitch's scale is the equivalent of BA1 On Moody's (MC).

As can be seen below, 90% of JNK's bonds are rated lower than that.

So while JNK appears to give you more yield, it does wade into far lower quality securities than ET.PR.I.

iShares BB Rated Corporate Bond ETF (HYBB) is another one we can use and this one comes closer to ET.PR.I's rating. This one uses the S&P/Fitch scale but unfortunately does not break down BB+, BB and BB-.

If you assume that the average credit quality is BB, you do can compare the yield to maturity versus ET.PR.I's current yield.

Sure, with ET.PR.I, you have a lot more duration risk, but you are getting paid for that, and it comes with a higher credit quality.

Alternatives

Energy Transfer issued subordinated bonds in 2024, and they are rated the same as the preferred shares. They are an interesting alternative for those that really don't want a K-1.

While these have a maturity date of 2054, they can be redeemed on October 1, 2029. If not redeemed, their interest payment resets as described below.

From and including the Settlement Date to, but excluding, October 1, 2029 (the “First Reset Date”), 7.125% per annum. From and including the First Reset Date, during each Reset Period (as defined in the Preliminary Prospectus), a per annum rate equal to the Five-year U.S. Treasury Rate (as defined in the Preliminary Prospectus) as of the most recent Reset Interest Determination Date (as defined in the Preliminary Prospectus), plus a spread equal to 2.829%, to be reset on each Reset Date.

These currently trade at $98.875 and have a yield to call of 7.3%.

While the yield is similar, keep in mind that these can be called fairly soon.

Investors who love Energy Transfer and love investment grade bonds, also have a compelling choice. They can buy the regular bonds (which are rated BBB) maturing on April 1, 2055. These trade at 93.5 cents on the dollar and have a coupon of 6.2%. The yield to maturity is 6.7%.

This is a downgrade from the ET.PR.I yield, but these are of course safer as they are higher in the capital stack. They also offer a similar upside in case of return of ZIRP, in which case they could trade up 20% higher (assuming ZIRP comes quickly.)

Verdict

While we cannot own ET.PR.I, it is held in our Conservative Income Portfolio in our Investing Group. We have been emphasizing fixed income recently and are aiming for about 80% in fixed income securities by the end of 2025. ET.PR.I is one of the better exchange traded ones available, and we would add to that position if it trades below $10.75. But we wouldn't get too excited over it or chase it much higher. The non-callable nature is really not helpful here, and there are many better securities which can give you about 7% or higher in yield.

Please note that this is not financial advice. It may seem like it, sound like it, but surprisingly, it is not. Investors are expected to do their own due diligence and consult a professional who knows their objectives and constraints. Most of our articles are not written for "buy and hold" investors. Our articles are not written for investors who ignore capital preservation while focusing only on yield.
